Experiment_description: Initial implementation of Checkpoint Trajectory Forecasting (CTF) systems to predict neural network final test accuracy from early training checkpoints. Two main approaches were compared: (1) a learned weight encoder that processes flattened checkpoint weights, and (2) a hand-crafted feature approach using weight statistics (mean, standard deviation, norm per layer). Both use LSTM to process sequences of checkpoint embeddings. Experiments trained small MLPs on MNIST with varied hyperparameters to generate training trajectories.
Significance: These experiments establish baseline CTF implementations and reveal a critical insight about feature representation for weight-space modeling. The finding that simple statistical summaries of weights significantly outperform learned encoders has important implications for practical early stopping systems and hyperparameter optimization, suggesting that interpretable hand-crafted features may be preferable when training data (trajectories) is limited.
Description: The experiments generated synthetic training trajectories by training small MLPs on MNIST with different hyperparameters (learning rates, hidden sizes), saving checkpoints at regular intervals. Two encoding approaches were tested: (1) A learned weight encoder processing flattened weights with ~50 trajectories, and (2) A statistics-based encoder computing mean, std, and norm per layer. Both approaches used LSTM networks to process checkpoint sequences, training on only the first 20% of checkpoints to predict final test accuracy. Evaluation used Pearson correlation between predicted and actual final accuracies, with train/validation/test splits to assess generalization.

Key_numerical_results.description: Test forecast correlation using hand-crafted weight statistics (mean, std, norm) encoder with LSTM
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Strong correlation demonstrates that simple statistical summaries of checkpoint weights capture sufficient information to predict final training outcomes from early checkpoints (first 20% of training).

Key_numerical_results.description: Test forecast correlation using learned weight encoder with LSTM
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Significantly lower correlation compared to statistics-based approach indicates overfitting of the learned encoder with limited training trajectories (~50). The learned approach fails to generalize despite achieving near-zero training loss.

Key_numerical_results.description: Test forecast correlation for single checkpoint baseline
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Negative correlation from single checkpoint prediction confirms that temporal trajectory information is essential for accurate forecasting, validating the core CTF hypothesis.

Key_numerical_results.description: Validation loss (MSE) for statistics-based CTF model
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Low validation loss close to training loss (0.0007) indicates good generalization of the statistics-based approach, in contrast to the learned encoder which showed severe train-validation gap.

Key_numerical_results.description: Validation loss for learned encoder CTF model (vs training loss ~0)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Large gap between training and validation loss reveals severe overfitting in the learned encoder approach, explaining its poor test correlation despite perfect training fit.
